stem: The range of \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \) is an interval of the form \( [k, \infty) \). What is \( k \)?
solution: Since \( [[a]] > 0 \), the parabola opens upward and its minimum value is the lower end of the range. That minimum is \( f\!\left(-\dfrac{[[b]]}{2\cdot[[a]]}\right) = ([[c]]) - \dfrac{([[b]])^{2}}{4\cdot[[a]]} = [[answer]] \), so \( k = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the \( x \)-coordinate of the vertex, not the minimum value of \( f \).
This reports the constant term, ignoring the \( -\frac{b^{2}}{4a} \) correction.
This adds the vertex correction instead of subtracting it.
This forgets the factor of \( [[a]] \) in the denominator \( 4a \).
This is off by one from the correct minimum.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.

stem: What real number is NOT in the range of \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x - ([[h]])} + ([[k]]) \)?
solution: Since \( \dfrac{1}{x-([[h]])} \) can never equal \( 0 \) for any real \( x \), \( f(x) \) can never equal \( [[k]] \); every other real number IS attained. So the excluded value is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the excluded \( x \)-value (where \( f \) is undefined), not the excluded output value.
This negates the correct excluded value.
This adds the horizontal and vertical shifts together, which has no bearing on the excluded output.
This subtracts the horizontal shift from the vertical one, which has no bearing on the excluded output.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The range of \( f(x) = [[a]]\sqrt{[[b]]x + ([[c]])} + ([[d]]) \) is bounded on exactly one side by a value \( M \). What is \( M \)?
solution: Since \( \sqrt{[[b]]x + ([[c]])} \ge 0 \) always, \( [[a]]\sqrt{[[b]]x+([[c]])} \) takes every value with the same sign as \( [[a]] \) (or zero), so \( f(x) \) is bounded on one side by \( M = [[answer]] \) (attained when the radicand is \( 0 \)).
distractor_rationales: This reports the radicand's constant term instead of the vertical shift that actually bounds the range.
This negates the correct boundary value.
This adds the leading coefficient \( [[a]] \) to the vertical shift for no valid reason.
This adds the radicand's constant term to the vertical shift, which has no bearing on the range.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \), restricted to the domain \( [\,[[m]], [[n]]\,] \). What is the minimum value of \( f \) on this domain?
solution: The unrestricted vertex is at \( x = -\dfrac{[[b]]}{2\cdot[[a]]} \). If that point lies in \( [\,[[m]],[[n]]\,] \), the minimum is the vertex value; otherwise the minimum occurs at whichever endpoint is closer to the vertex. Evaluating \( f \) at the vertex clamped into \( [\,[[m]],[[n]]\,] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This takes the smaller of the two ENDPOINT values, which is wrong whenever the true vertex lies inside the domain and dips below both endpoints.
This uses the unrestricted global vertex value even though the vertex may fall outside \( [\,[[m]],[[n]]\,] \), where it is not attainable.
This always uses the left endpoint, ignoring where the true vertex actually falls relative to the domain.
This always uses the right endpoint, ignoring where the true vertex actually falls relative to the domain.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.

stem: What is the smallest integer in the range of \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \)?
solution: The minimum value of \( f \) is \( ([[c]]) - \dfrac{([[b]])^{2}}{4\cdot[[a]]} \), which is not an integer here. Since the range is \( [\text{min}, \infty) \), the smallest integer actually attained is the next integer at or above the minimum: \( \left\lceil ([[c]]) - \dfrac{([[b]])^{2}}{4\cdot[[a]]} \right\rceil = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This rounds down instead of up, giving an integer below the actual minimum that is never attained.
This reports the exact (non-integer) minimum rather than the smallest integer in the range.
This gives the \( x \)-coordinate of the vertex, not a value in the range at all.
This reports the constant term, ignoring the vertex correction entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: What is the maximum value of \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]])} \)?
solution: Since the denominator \( x^{2}+([[B]])x+([[C]]) \) is always positive (its discriminant is negative), \( f \) is maximized exactly where the denominator is minimized. That minimum is \( ([[C]]) - \dfrac{([[B]])^{2}}{4} \), so the maximum of \( f \) is \( \dfrac{1}{([[C]]) - ([[B]])^{2}/4} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the minimum of the denominator itself instead of taking its reciprocal.
This uses the constant term alone instead of the true minimum of the denominator.
This negates the correct maximum.
This adds instead of subtracts the vertex correction when finding the denominator's minimum.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
